questions.text: I hold the view that my time belongs as much to others as it does to me.
I believe that the willingness to take suffering seriously — to let it weigh on me — is an expression of who I fundamentally am.
I am attracted to opportunities where I can set the tone for a team.
I feel that building a legacy around my own achievements is a meaningful aspiration.
My emotional responses tend to linger long after the triggering event has passed.
I believe that an organized environment reflects and supports an organized mind.
I experience anticipatory anxiety as a full-body state, not just a mental worry.
I find that planning reduces stress because I know what to expect.
I believe that maintaining a measured, objective posture during emotionally intense events is a form of service that is rarely recognized but always valuable.
I believe that agreement among the group matters more than speed.
I sense when someone has rehearsed what they are about to say.
I pick up on when someone shortens their responses as a signal they want the conversation to end.
I am comfortable making unpopular decisions if I believe they are correct.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the reality that reveals the seriousness of ethics in a way that no theoretical argument can match.
My emotional responses are like a well-tuned instrument — responsive but never distorted.
I feel most at peace when I know I have put others before myself.
I do not pick up on the nonverbal signals that indicate whether a group wants my input.
I don't dwell on things I've forgotten to do; I simply pick them up when I remember.
